title: Bleeding

steps.title: Ensure safety & protect yourself
steps.instruction: Make sure the scene is safe. Put on disposable gloves or use a plastic bag to avoid contact with blood. If unavailable, ask the person to apply pressure themselves if able.

steps.title: Call for help
steps.instruction: Call 101 immediately if the wound is deep, blood is spurting or flowing continuously, or the amount of blood equals half a soda can or more. Don't move the person unless necessary.

steps.title: Find the source of bleeding
steps.instruction: Remove clothing around the wound to locate the source. There may be more than one injury. Remove obvious debris only — do NOT probe the wound or remove large embedded objects.

steps.title: Apply direct pressure
steps.instruction: Cover the wound with sterile gauze or a clean cloth. Press firmly and steadily with the palm of your hand. Keep the injured area on a firm surface. Do NOT press on an embedded object — press around it instead.

steps.title: Keep the pressure on
steps.instruction: Hold firm pressure continuously until bleeding stops. If blood soaks through, add more gauze on top — do NOT remove the original dressing. Do not stack multiple layers — replace the top layer only.

steps.title: Elevate and bandage
steps.instruction: If possible, raise the wounded area above the level of the heart to slow bleeding. Once bleeding stops, wrap the wound with a thick bandage or clean cloth and secure it firmly.

steps.title: Help the person lie down
steps.instruction: Have the person lie down on a blanket or rug to prevent body heat loss. If you see signs of shock — pale or clammy skin, rapid weak pulse, dizziness — elevate their feet and keep them calm and still.

steps.title: Use a tourniquet if needed
steps.instruction: If bleeding is life-threatening and on an arm or leg, apply a commercial tourniquet above the wound if available and you are trained. Do NOT use improvised tourniquets like scarves or belts. Note the time it was applied and tell emergency services.

steps.title: Monitor until help arrives
steps.instruction: Stay with the person. Watch for changes in breathing, responsiveness, or signs of shock. Keep them warm and reassure them. Wash your hands thoroughly after giving care.

warnings: Do NOT remove embedded objects from the wound
Do NOT remove the original dressing — add on top if soaked
Do NOT use improvised tourniquets like scarves or belts
Do NOT press directly on a head wound if skull fracture is suspected
sources: American Red Cross First Aid Guidelines
